mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 116d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 116d engine typically costs £1,800–£3,500 supply only depending on generation, with B37 engines commanding a premium over N47 units. Fitting adds £650–£1,300. A used B37 engine starts from around £1,615, while a reconditioned N47D16A costs approximately £2,339–£3,021. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

It depends entirely on which engine generation your 116d has — this badge spans the N47 (2007–2015, the well-documented rear-chain risk generation) and the B37 (2015–present, timing chain relocated to the front, meaningfully improved). The B37 is widely regarded as a solid, smooth and quiet engine. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 116d?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain wear on N47-engined cars (2007–2015), EGR cooler failure affecting both N47 and B37 engines (subject to BMW recall), swirl flap actuator failure on B37 engines, and injector issues on early N47s.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 116d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept £5,000–£8,000 116d, a £2,500 recon is 30–50% of the car's value — worth it. Against a tired £3,000 example, the same recon exceeds our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 116d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: pre-2015 is N47 (four-cylinder, rear-chain), 2015-onwards is B37 (three-cylinder, front-chain). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N47 timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative chain replacement (£650–£1,800) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain snap (£1,800–£4,500 engine replacement). At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately.

eraMap.title: BMW 116d — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: Four-cylinder — rear-mounted timing chain, well-documented failure risk
Revised chain tensioner but same fundamental rear-chain design
Three-cylinder — chain relocated to front, meaningfully more reliable
Front-wheel-drive platform — same B37 engine, improved packaging

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current market for N47 and B37 engines combined. B37 reconditioned engines typically command a premium due to their newer design and lower failure rate. A reconditioned N47D16A engine costs approximately £2,339–£3,021, while a used B37D15A engine with low mileage starts from around £1,615. A B37 reconditioned engine with fitting is available from approximately £1,995. Older E8x N47 engines: see the Era Map above for E81/E87-specific pricing, which runs lower.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 116d engine typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The rear-mounted chain on N47 engines makes timing-related labour more expensive.

cta.label: → Compare 116d engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: N47 Rear Timing Chain Failure (E81/E87, F20/F21 pre-LCI)
problems.symptoms: Cold-start rattle lasting 1–30 seconds, metallic ticking from the rear of the engine (against the firewall), engine warning light, loss of power

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: This is the single most searched 116d issue for good reason. The N47's timing chain is mounted at the rear of the engine, against the firewall, making access extremely difficult and labour-intensive. The chain, guides and tensioners were poorly designed and constructed. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the chain inspected immediately. A £650–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £1,800–£4,500 replacement that follows a snapped chain. The N47 timing chain issue was never definitively fixed across the engine's production run.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: EGR Cooler Failure (N47 and B37, all generations)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, white exhaust smoke, occasional burning smell, engine warning light
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — subject to BMW recall on many vehicles
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)
problems.recommendation: Lots of diesel BMWs have had recalls for EGR problems. The EGR cooler is a known vulnerability across both N47 and B37 engines. One 116d owner reported: "EGR cooler fail - repaired via recall". Verify recall completion before buying any 116d — this is a straightforward, well-documented fix when caught early, but ignored coolant loss can lead to overheating damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full EGR cooler failure guide

problems.title: Swirl Flap Actuator Failure (B37, F20/F21 LCI and F40)
problems.symptoms: Ticking noise from the engine bay, hard starting, poor performance at low revs, limp mode, check engine light
problems.repairCost: £300–£1,000+ (swirl flap actuator repair or intake manifold replacement)
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not typically progress to engine replacement)

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if symptoms appear

problems.recommendation: The B37 three-cylinder diesel can suffer from swirl flap actuator failure. One owner reported: "they told me the swirl flap rod has gone and it'll need a new intake manifold £1000+ all in". The swirl flap system aids cylinder combustion and efficiency on diesels, but the mechanical linkage can wear and break. A swirl flap delete can offer long-term reliability at the cost of some low-down torque. If you experience ticking or poor low-end performance, have the swirl flap system inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full swirl flap failure guide

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 116d? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2015 B37-engined car (timing chain relocated to the front — the cleaner slate)
* Documented timing chain history if buying an N47-engined pre-LCI car
* Full service history with sub-15,000km oil intervals
avoidIf: * N47-engined car (pre-2015) with cold-start rattle and no chain history — this is a ticking time bomb
* No proof of EGR recall work
* Poor service history on any generation — diesel BMWs are unforgiving of neglect
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: Across the 116d's lifetime, the honest advice is simple: buy a B37 (2015-onwards) if you want the cleanest slate — the three-cylinder engine is smoother, quieter, and fundamentally more reliable than the N47 it replaced. The N47's rear-mounted timing chain is a genuine weak point that BMW never fully resolved. Buy on documented history, not on price alone.

cta.label: → Compare 116d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 116d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for 1–30 seconds of rattle (timing chain warning, N47 engines)
* Service History — Including which engine era (N47 or B37) and confirmed oil-interval discipline
* EGR Recall — Verify EGR cooler recall completion — this affects both N47 and B37
* DPF Health — Check for DPF warning lights; short journeys accelerate wear on all diesels
* Swirl Flap System — Listen for ticking; check for low-end power loss (B37 engines)
* Injector Health — Any rough idle or misfire? Injector failure is documented on N47
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
